body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

long-agentic-session-stress asserts that the daemon keeps a long, tool-heavy session coherently observed: state stays continuously `working` across MANY interleaved assistant-message / tool-call / tool-result iterations, with no spurious flicker to ready or waiting mid-work, and a clean settle to ready at the end. The user-observable signal is the single sustained working span plus the final settle — not tool-event internals or exact tool counts. Distinct from self-correction-iteration (a few retry cycles within one turn) and autonomous-loop (self-prompted, no user prompt): here ONE user prompt kicks off a deliberately long multi-step agentic burst that chains a high volume of tool calls before settling.

OpenCode supports tool calls (capabilities.json tool_calls:true). In headless default (`opencode run`) tools auto-execute inside OpenCode's own sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt, so a high-volume multi-tool single-turn run produces exactly the ungated, contiguous ready→working→ready arc with no waiting. Session state lives in OpenCode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db); the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the UUID-keyed ses_… session.

Each tool call lands in the part table as a `tool` part progressing pending → running → completed/error; the opencode parser maps it to function_call (pending/running) and function_call_output (completed/error) events (parser.go parseToolPart), and the turn settles working→ready only when the parser sees a step-finish part with reason="stop" → turn_done (parser.go parseStepFinish). Crucially, a tool-calls step-finish reason keeps the session in working (mid-turn pause), so a long sequence of write→read→edit tool parts inside one turn is ONE sustained working span — exactly the stress this scenario names: the parser must stay coherent across a large volume of interleaved parts without prematurely flipping ready. The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the many tool calls are asserted via the single sustained working span plus the no-waiting invariant, and the high tool volume is verified out-of-band in the SQLite store (≥5 tool parts before turn_done).

WHY ONE multi-step turn rather than four separate user-driven turns: opencode's driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) runs a blocking `opencode run` per send (not a held-open REPL). Rapid back-to-back same-session sends coalesce in the daemon's poller, blurring the per-turn arcs, and the local gemma 2B model is slow (~30–45s per turn/tool round) so multi-turn runs balloon. A single crisp multi-step prompt (create step1..stepN files, write into each, read them all back, write a summary) makes opencode's agent loop chain many tool calls in one sustained `opencode run` subprocess lifetime — capturing the long contiguous working span the daemon must keep coherent, which is the load-bearing observable. This mirrors the proven sibling opencode arc-only specs (auto-executed-tool-call, self-correction-iteration).

MODEL note: opencode's default model is the local LM Studio model lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored (set in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json). The driver runs blocking `opencode run` with NO --model flag, so it uses that default. gemma is a small (2B) model that is slow and may be unreliable at sustaining a LONG multi-tool loop in one turn; the recipe uses a crisp, fully-enumerated multi-step task to force several write→read tool calls into one working span. If gemma simply will not sustain the loop / will not emit the tool calls this becomes an infra/MODEL limitation, not an opencode/daemon incapability (capabilities.json tool_calls:true).

The driver runs a blocking `opencode run` per turn; one send + wait_turn (a no-op) + a trailing sleep is within its grammar, so driver_capability: ready. A single send carries the whole multi-tool turn, so the contiguous working span is captured in one `opencode run` subprocess lifetime.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the ses_… session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.

The local gemma 2B model is slow and may be unreliable at sustaining a LONG multi-tool loop in one turn; the cell records normally if it sustains the burst (≥5 tool parts), otherwise the limitation is the model, not opencode (capabilities.json tool_calls:true).

The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the stress is asserted via the single sustained working span + the no-waiting invariant; the high tool-call volume is verified in the SQLite store / transcript out-of-band at record time.

Recorded as ONE multi-step turn rather than many separate user-driven turns: opencode's per-send blocking `opencode run` plus the daemon poller coalesce rapid back-to-back same-session sends, so the long contiguous working span is the deterministic, load-bearing observable.

sources.note: parseToolPart: tool part status pending/running → function_call (ToolUses), completed/error → function_call_output (ToolResultIDs); parseStepFinish: reason="stop" → turn_done settles working→ready, while a tool-calls step-finish reason keeps the session working — so a long multi-tool single-turn run is one contiguous working span the parser must keep coherent.
tool_calls:true; opencode can invoke tools. Headless default `opencode run` auto-executes tools with no interactive gate, so the high-volume burst never enters waiting.
Source variant ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) with ExactName{Name:"opencode"}; daemon polls the session table for the ses_… session.

Sibling committed opencode spec proving the ProcessOwnedStore ready→working→ready arc with MULTIPLE tool calls inside one continuous working span, session_birth anchored UNPINNED to start and the no-waiting invariant — the exact shape this cell extends with a HIGHER volume of tool calls in the single sustained working span.
